name: Equal Groups and Fair Shares — Before Times and Divide
whatIsIt: Making EQUAL GROUPS is the seed of multiplication: 3 baskets with 4 apples each is 4 + 4 + 4 = 12. FAIR SHARING is the seed of division: 12 sweets shared equally among 3 friends is 4 each. Same picture, read two ways — group up, or share out.
whyNeeded: Whenever we count in groups (pairs of shoes, wheels on cars) or share fairly (sweets, slices), we are getting ready for multiplication and division. Doing it with real objects first makes those big words easy later.
realLifeUses: Counting wheels: cars in 2s, autos in 3s
Packing eggs in trays of equal rows
Making equal teams for a game

vocabulary.meaning: Groups that each have the same number.
How many altogether — add the groups or count all.
Splitting into equal parts so everyone gets the same.

story.title: The Sweet Shop Trays
story.text: At the sweet shop, Bhaiya fills 3 trays with 4 laddoos each. 'How many laddoos in all?' he asks Nisha. She counts in groups: 4, 8, 12. 'Twelve!' Then a customer buys all 12 and shares them fairly among 3 children. 'Four each,' says Nisha — 'sharing is just grouping backwards!'
story.extractedProblem: 3 trays with 4 laddoos each — how many laddoos in all?

visual.caption: 3 equal groups of 4 make 12 in all. Share 12 among 3 → 4 each.

teachingGallery.title: Group up or share out
teachingGallery.note: Equal groups add to a total; fair sharing splits a total equally.

standardMethod.summary: For 'in all', add the equal groups (or skip-count). For 'fair share', deal one to each until none are left, then count one share.
steps: Equal groups → total: skip-count the group size (4, 8, 12) or add 4 + 4 + 4.
Fair share → each: deal out one to each person, round and round, until none are left.
Count how many ONE person got — that is the share.

workedExamples.problem: There are 5 autos, each with 3 wheels. How many wheels in all?
12 biscuits are shared equally among 4 children. How many does each child get?
steps: Deal one to each, round and round.

commonMistakes.mistake: Making groups that are not equal, then counting them as if they were.
commonMistakes.fix: Every group must have the SAME number before you skip-count or share. Check each group first.

commonMistakes.mistake: Sharing so one person gets more than another.
commonMistakes.fix: Fair sharing means EVERYONE gets the same. Deal one at a time to each until none are left.

easy.q: 3 baskets with 2 apples each. How many apples in all?
To count equal groups quickly you can…

medium.q: 4 cars, each with 4 wheels. How many wheels in all?
10 sweets shared equally among 2 friends. How many each?
hintLadder: Deal one to each, round and round.

challenge.q: 15 mangoes shared equally among 3 baskets. How many in each basket?
Raj shares 8 toffees so he gets 5 and his sister gets 3. Is this a fair share?

options.label: No — a fair share gives 4 and 4

challenge.answer: No — a fair share gives 4 and 4

questions.q: 2 trays with 5 laddoos each. How many in all?
3 groups of 4 stars. How many stars in all?
12 crayons shared equally among 3 children. How many each?
Before you skip-count groups, you must make sure the groups are…

options.label: Equal — the same number in each

questions.answer: Equal — the same number in each

teachBackPrompt: Use real objects (buttons, coins or biscuits). First make equal groups and tell a grown-up the total by skip-counting. Then share the same objects fairly among a few people and say how many each gets — explaining why grouping and sharing are opposites.

revisionCard.summary: Equal groups → total: skip-count the group size (4, 8, 12). Fair share → each: deal one at a time to everyone until none are left, then count one share. Groups and shares must always be EQUAL.

rememberIt.hook: Equal groups -> multiplication. '3 groups of 4' = 3 x 4
rememberIt.unpack: Multiplication is fast adding of EQUAL groups. 4 + 4 + 4 = 3 x 4 = 12. The 'x' means 'groups of'.

studentQuestions.a: 3 groups of 4 (or 4 added 3 times): 4+4+4 = 12. Multiplication is quick repeated adding.
Yes! 3 groups of 4 and 4 groups of 3 both make 12. You can swap them.
